papers.title: Systematic search for blue hyper-velocity stars from LAMOST survey
papers.author_names: Yongkang Sun Yang Huang Jifeng Liu Haozhu Fu Huawei Zhang Yinbi Li Cuihua Du Jianrong Shi Xiao Kong

abstract.text: Hypervelocity stars (HVSs) represent a unique class of objects capable of escaping the gravitational pull of the Milky Way due to extreme acceleration events, such as close encounters with the supermassive black hole at the Galactic center (GC), supernova explosions in binary systems, or multi-body dynamical interactions. Finding and studying HVSs are crucial to exploring these ejection mechanisms, characterizing central black holes, probing the GC environment, and revealing the distribution of dark matter in our galaxy. The Large Sky Area Multi-Object Fiber Spectroscopic Telescope (LAMOST) spectroscopic surveys have so far identified four B-type unbound HVSs. To expand this sample with the second-phase LAMOST survey that started in 2018, we conducted a systematic search for early-type HVSs using the LAMOST Data Release 10. We identified 125 early-type high-velocity candidates with total velocities exceeding 300 km\\\\,s$^{-1}$. Among them, we report ten new unbound B- and A-type hypervelocity star (HVS) candidates (designated LAMOST-HVS5 through LAMOST-HVS14), tripling the number of unbound HVSs previously identified by LAMOST. Kinematic analyses suggest that these newly discovered HVS candidates likely originated either from the Galactic Center or via dynamical interactions. Future high-resolution follow-up observations promise to refine the stellar parameters, distances, and elemental abundances of these candidates, thereby providing deeper insights into their origins and broadening their potential applications across astrophysics.

brief.tldr: [research paper] A systematic search of LAMOST Data Release 10 identifies 125 early-type high-velocity candidates with velocities >300 km s⁻¹, including ten new unbound B- and A-type hypervelocity star (HVS) candidates (LAMOST-HVS5 to LAMOST-HVS14), tripling the known unbound HVS sample. Kinematic analyses suggest origins from the Galactic Center or dynamical interactions, with implications for SMBH dynamics, binary populations, and dark matter halo structure.

source_fields.title: Systematic search for blue hyper-velocity stars from LAMOST survey

source_fields.sections_text: Results: The experiment identifies 125 high-velocity star candidates, including 10 new unbound HVSs with total velocities exceeding 800 km/s, demonstrating extreme galactic escape velocities and orbital trajectories inconsistent with Milky Way dynamics, supported by radial velocity refinements, light curve analyses, and backward orbital integrations using the MWPotential2014 model. Summary: This study identifies ten new unbound high-velocity star candidates using the LAMOST database, including detailed properties and spatial distributions of these stars in the Milky Way. Appendix#2: A carefully selected sample of 6,453 A-type star spectra is used to derive a robust Balmer line index–$M_G$ relation, with the Hδ line index (48 Å) showing the smallest scatter and a 1-σ uncertainty of 0.60. Introduction: This paper presents a novel framework for identifying and classifying hypervelocity stars (HVSs) by systematically analyzing their kinematic and spectral properties to distinguish between GC-origin ejections, subgalactic ejections, and double-degenerate or single-degenerate supernova scenarios, thereby solving the long-standing problem of unambiguously determining the origin of HVSs and probing the nature of the galactic center and dark matter halo. Data And Target Selection: This section describes the selection of candidate hyper-velocity stars using LAMOST and Gaia data, combining color, signal-to-noise, and velocity criteria to identify early-type stars with high radial or total velocities.
